does: This paper identifies and addresses the unconditional preference problem that arises when applying direct preference optimization (DPO) to multimodal language models. The authors propose mDPO, which modifies the DPO objective to explicitly optimize both image and language preferences rather than treating preferences unconditionally. A key technical contribution is introducing a reward anchor mechanism that ensures positive rewards for chosen responses, preventing the unintended likelihood decrease that occurs in relative preference optimization methods. The approach is evaluated on two multimodal LLMs of different scales across three benchmarks, showing consistent improvements in performance and substantial reductions in hallucination errors compared to standard DPO application.
connects: This work builds on direct preference optimization, a recent alignment method for LLMs that learns from preference data without requiring reinforcement learning. The paper extends DPO to the multimodal setting, where previous attempts to naively apply DPO struggled because models would ignore image conditions and optimize only language preferences. It responds to the challenge of aligning vision-language models, which have proven difficult to fine-tune with existing preference-based methods. The reward anchor mechanism draws on insights from preference optimization literature about the role of absolute reward scaling in relative preference methods, adapting these insights to the multimodal case where conditioning on visual inputs is critical.
enables: This work opens directions for more effective alignment of multimodal models through better preference optimization techniques. By successfully addressing the conditioning problem, it enables future research into more nuanced preference learning that accounts for multiple modalities simultaneously. The reward anchor mechanism could inspire similar conditioning improvements in other relative preference optimization schemes beyond the multimodal setting. The approach provides a foundation for scaling preference optimization to larger multimodal models and for developing methods that better handle hallucination in vision-language systems. It also suggests that many ineffective applications of LLM alignment methods to multimodal settings may stem from overlooked conditioning issues, potentially guiding improvements in other areas like video understanding or cross-modal reasoning tasks.
